coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated with maleimide and zinc tetramaleimido-phthalocyanine: Application in the design of “OFF-ON” fluorescence sensors for biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of maleimide-derivatized graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) and zinc phthalocyanine (2) as novel sensor probes for the selective detection of biothiols (cysteine, homocysteine or glutathione) through the rapid and specific Michael addition reaction between biothiols and the maleimide-derivatized probes is presented in this study. GQDs directly functionalized with maleimide units (M-GQDs) were synthesized and deployed for biothiols recognition following the principle of Michael addition. M-GQDs probe was found to be highly sensitive and selective towards biothiols detection in the nanomolar range in aqueous solution and at physiological pH (7.0). On the other hand, non-covalent interaction between pristine GQDs and novel zinc tetramaleimido-derivatized phthalocyanine resulted in the quenching of the pristine GQDs fluorescence emission which was switched back to the “ON” mode by Michael addition mechanism in the presence of biothiols. Tested relevant biomolecules did not interfere in the quantitative recognition of the biothiols. The probes showed to be highly sensitive, specific and selective for biothiols sensing in simulated real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Maleimide
Zinc tetramaleimido-phthalocyanine
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescence “turn ON”
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing procedures for the biothiols (Cys, Hcy and GSH)
Characterization of pristine GQDs-zinc tetramaleimido Pc analogue (2-GQDs)
Application of the maleimide-derivatized probes for biothiols detection
Recovery studies (analytical applicability of the probes)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Overlayed survey scans XPS spectra of as-synthesized M-GQDs and PEI-GQDs, (B) High resolution spectrum of N1s of PEI-GQDs (a) M-GQDs (b), and (C) High resolution spectrum of C1s of PEI-GQDs (a) and M-GQDs (b).
(A) AFM image of as-synthesized M-GQDs (Height line profile shown below image). (B and C) TEM and DLS graph of as synthesized M-GQDs. (D and E) TEM and DLS graph of 2-GQDs.
FTIR spectra of PEI-GQDs (a), M-GQDs (b) and 4-maleimidobenzoic acid (c).
Raman spectra of M-GQDs (black) and PEI-GQDs (blue).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-GQDs alone and upon attachment of maleimide units to form M-GQDs at 350nm excitation wavelength. (B) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs and 2-GQDs showing the quenching process (340 excitation wavelength). Solvent: PBS (1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) for A and DMSO/PBS (1:4v/v, 1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) for B. Inset in A: Normalized emission spectra of PEI-GQDs and M-GQDs showing the spectral shift.
FTIR spectra of complex 1 (a), complex 2 (b), pristine GQDs (c) and 2-GQDs conjugate (d).
UV/vis absorption spectra of complexes 1 (a), 2 (b), 2-GQDs (c) and pristine GQDs (d). DMSO/PBS (1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0).

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) M-GQDs alone (0.1mgmL−1), and in the presence of 50nmol L−1 of Cys, Hcy or GSH and (B) M-GQDs “turn-ON” signals at different concentrations of Cys. Solvent: PBS (1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0). λexc =350nm. Inset of B: calibration plot.
2-GQDs “turn-ON” signals at different concentrations of Cys. Solvent: DMSO/PBS (1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0). λexc =340nm. Inset: calibration plot. [2-GQDs] =0.5mgmL−1.

(A) UV–vis spectral changes of 2 alone in the presence of different amounts of Cys (50 and 100nmolL−1) and (B) Fluorescence decay curves showing changes in lifetimes GQDs component of the 2-GQDs probe (0.5mgmL−1) before and after addition of Cys. Solvent is DMSO/PBS (1:4) (PBS, 1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0).

Selectivity of the biosensor 2-GQDs (0.5mgmL−1) in DMSO/PBS (1:4) in the presence of other α-amino acids and proteins (at tolerable error level of 5% and three replicate measurements, n=3). Cys (120nmolL−1), Hcy (150nmolL−1) or GSH (200nmolL−1). a-k (1.0µmolL−1): Glycine, lysine, histidine, tryptophan, trypsin, cystine, alanine, lactic acid, bovine serum albumin, cytochrome c, lysozyme, respectively.
Synthetic route for the preparation of maleimide-functionalized graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) via EDC/NHS chemistry.
Synthetic route for the preparation of maleimide-derivatized zinc phthalocyanine (Pc). Complex 1: zinc tetraamino Pc. Complex 2: zinc tetramaleimido Pc.
Schematic illustration of π-π stacking of GQDs and maleimide-derivatized ZnPc (2) and biothiols sensing by the GQDs-maleimide-derivatized ZnPc (2-GQDs) conjugate.
Mechanistic pathways for off-on switching of M-GQDs fluorescence by biothiols.

Optical and fluorescence parameters of M-GQDs, pristine GQDs and 2-GQDs. Mean size from DLS experiments also shown. DMSO/PBS 1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) for 2-QGDs and PBS (1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) for M-GQDs and pristine GQDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: values in bracket are emission maxima wavelength (excitation at 350nm).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: values in brackets are for excitation where 2 absorbs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the sensitivity of M-GQDs and 2-GQDs novel nanoprobes with some reported analytical techniques for the detection of biothiols.

row.entry: Graphene oxide and Ru-complex, fluorescence switch-OFF

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: values in brackets are the linear ranges. Units in nmolL−1 unless otherwise stated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery studies of biothiols (Cys shown as an example) of the designed nanoprobes for biothiols quantification in simulated samples demonstrating analytical applicability, reproducibility and stability of the nanoprobes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated with maleimide and zinc tetramaleimido-phthalocyanine: Application in the design of “OFF-ON” fluorescence sensors for biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, P.O. Box 94, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of maleimide-derivatized graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) and zinc phthalocyanine (2) as novel sensor probes for the selective detection of biothiols (cysteine, homocysteine or glutathione) through the rapid and specific Michael addition reaction between biothiols and the maleimide-derivatized probes is presented in this study. GQDs directly functionalized with maleimide units (M-GQDs) were synthesized and deployed for biothiols recognition following the principle of Michael addition. M-GQDs probe was found to be highly sensitive and selective towards biothiols detection in the nanomolar range in aqueous solution and at physiological pH (7.0). On the other hand, non-covalent interaction between pristine GQDs and novel zinc tetramaleimido-derivatized phthalocyanine resulted in the quenching of the pristine GQDs fluorescence emission which was switched back to the “ON” mode by Michael addition mechanism in the presence of biothiols. Tested relevant biomolecules did not interfere in the quantitative recognition of the biothiols. The probes showed to be highly sensitive, specific and selective for biothiols sensing in simulated real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The deployment of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in biosensing is mainly due to their fascinating optical properties, non-toxicity and biocompatibility [1,2]. GQDs show excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties which may be influenced by structural defects and surface functionalization (such as doping with heteroatoms) [3–6]. In addition to the photoluminescence properties, excellent water solubility and high photo-stability are other interesting properties exhibited by GQDs. Hence, GQDs are considered as promising nanomaterials in applications such as fluorescence sensing, bio-imaging and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [2,7].

Over the years, the design and fabrication of nanoprobes capable of selective recognition and quantification of biomolecules has drawn a lot of attention due to the crucial roles played by these molecules in biological systems and in the environment [8,9]. Biothiols (such as L-cysteine (Cys), DL-homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH)) are of particular interest due to their key roles in cellular metabolisms. Fluorescence probes are attractive for sensing of biomolecules since they are sensitive, rapid, simple and cost effective [10]. Thus, fluorescence nanoprobes have been fabricated for biothiols detection based on a plethora of mechanisms [11]. For instance, fluorescent materials such as GQDs-Hg2+ blend, carbon nanodots, semiconductors QDs (CdTe, CdSe/ZnSe), and PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag nanoparticles blend (PEI = polyethyleneimine) have been developed for the detection of biothiols [12–14]. Maleimide-bearing fluorophores such as boron-dipyrromethenes (BODIPYs) [15], 1,8-naphthalic anhydride [16], 1,8-naphthalimide [17] and binaphthalene [18] have been reportedly employed as biothiols fluorescent probes based on the Michael addition (thiol-maleimide reaction) of biothiols to the maleimide-appended fluorophores, which is selective and rapid with the formation of stable thiol-maleimide products [19]. Thus, maleimide functionalized fluorophores have been the most successfully utilized thiol-reactive probes (for biothiols sensing) reported to date with commercial successes, due to the facile reaction between thiols and Michael acceptors (maleimides, for example) [11].

Due to the excellent electronic and optical properties of GQDs which makes them excellent sensor materials (as discussed above), we herein attached maleimide units (Michael acceptor) to graphene quantum dots ( Scheme 1) for biothiols detection (in aqueous solution and at physiological pH) via the Michael addition mechanism for the first time. It is expected that the linking of maleimide to GQDs will result in the quenching (“turn OFF”) of their fluorescence, which will then be enhanced (“turned ON”) in the presence of the biothiols resulting in the selective detection of the latter. On the other hand, metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) are macrocyclic aromatic compounds which are known for their excellent optical and electronic properties and are well known optical sensors [20]. As such, we envisaged that the anchoring of Michael acceptors such as maleimides to the terminals of MPcs could result in complexes which can be employed for specific interaction with biothiols via the Michael addition reaction. Thus in this work, novel maleimide-derivatized zinc phthalocyanine was synthesized for the first time by incorporating maleimide groups on the terminals of zinc phthalocyanine ring. Pcs with maleimides as ring substituents are not known and thus reported for the first time in this study. The metallophthalocyanine (MPc) employed in this study is zinc tetramaleimido Pc which possesses maleimide units directly on its ring (complex 2, Scheme 2), specifically designed to interact with biothiols. We show in this work that the sensing ability of complex 2 towards biothiols improves when it is non-covalently (π-π stacking) conjugated to simple (pristine) GQDs. The fluorescence of pristine GQDs was quenched by complex 2, but was “turned ON” by biothiols interaction with complex 2 via Michael addition mechanism. The Michael addition of the biothiols to the 2-GQDs probe could occur rapidly and selectively with complex 2 within the hybrid, resulting in the saturation (removal of π bonds) of the maleimide rings and disruption of the interaction between the pristine GQDs and complex 2 (Scheme 3). The performance of pristine GQDs conjugated to the maleimide bearing MPc (complex 2) as a probe is compared with GQDs directly functionalized with maleimide units (M-GQDs). This work forms part of our continuing work on the development of nanocomposites of GQDs and MPc complexes for specific sensing of different analytes [21]. To the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of maleimide-derivatized GQDs and Pc as attractive and promising probes for the quantitative sensing of biothiols is reported for the first time.

Zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) standard, quinine sulfate, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), zinc bromide, hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS), 4-maleimidobenzoic acid, benzene, magnesium sulfate, lysine, alanine, cystine and cytochrome c were obtained from Aldrich. Histidine, lactic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, trypsin, tryptophan were obtained from Fluka. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF), sodium hydrogen carbonate, maleic anhydride, deuterated DMSO-d 6 were obtained from Merck. Glycine, ethyl acetate and chloroform were purchased from SAARCHEM (South Africa). Column chromatography was performed using silica gel. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received. Pristine (GQDs) and polyethyleneimine grafted graphene quantum dots (PEI-GQDs) were synthesized following procedures reported elsewhere [14,21]. Zinc tetraamino phthalocyanine (ZnTAPc) (1) was synthesized according to procedures previously reported [22].

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Ground state electronic absorption was performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range of 300–800nm. Infra-red spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum-ATR. 1H NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker AVANCE 600MHz NMR spectrometer in DMSO-d 6. Elemental analyses were done using a Vario-Elementar Microcube ELIII. Mass spectra data were collected on a Bruker Auto-FLEX III Smart-beam MALDI-TOF/TOF mass spectrometer using α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid as the matrix in the positive ion mode. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 300, Picoquant GmbH) with a diode laser. Details have been provided before [21]. A Metrohm Swiss 827pH meter was used for pH measurements. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data as reported previously [21]. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurement in tapping mode was carried out with MFP-3D Origin supplied by Asylum research (Oxford instruments company, USA). M-GQDs sample for AFM was prepared by drop casting and room temperature drying of the analyte solution on a freshly cleaved mica surface.

Graphene quantum dots grafted with polyethyleneimine (PEI-GQDs) used as the precursor for M-GQDs preparation were synthesized as reported elsewhere [14]. M-GQDs preparation is described briefly (Scheme 1): In a typical experiment, the carboxylic groups of 4-maleimidobenzoic acid (0.1g, 0.46mmol were activated with 0.025g (0.13mmol) of EDC and 0.013g (0.11mmol) of NHS in phosphate buffered solution (PBS) (1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) under gentle stirring for 24h. Then 5mL of 2mgmL−1 of PEI-GQDs was added. The resulting mixture was left to further stir for another 24h at room temperature. After this time, 10mL of ethanol was added to the product and the resulting precipitate was collected by centrifugation and further purified by washing with a mixture of methanol and water (2:1) so as to dispel the unconjugated molecules and nanoparticles.

The synthesis of complex 2 was achieved by the conversion of the four terminal amino groups (-NH2) on complex 1 to maleimide groups following procedures described previously for the transformation of amino moiety to a maleimide moiety [23], with modifications. Briefly: 0.38g (0.6mmol) of 1 in 10mL of DMF was mixed with maleic anhydride (1g, 10.12mmol) in dry benzene (100mL). The mixture was thoroughly stirred at room temperature for 1h. The resulting mixture was further refluxed for 4h, followed by the addition of ZnBr2 (2.28g, 9.7mmol) in one portion, and the mixture was further refluxed for 1h. After this time, the mixture was allowed to cool and a solution of hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS) (2.75mL, 13.42mmol) in dry benzene (30mL) was added slowly over a period of 30min. Next, the mixture was refluxed for an additional 4h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting mixture was poured into 1.0molL−1 HCl (140mL). Liquid-liquid extraction procedure was employed to extract the aqueous phase using ethyl acetate. The organic phase recovered after the extraction was washed successively with 80mL of saturated NaHCO3 and 120mL of brine and then dried over anhydrous MgSO4. The crude product was concentrated using a rotary evaporator and further purification was done over a silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of chloroform and ethyl acetate (10:3) as the eluent solvent system to yield compound 2. Note that the yield of 2 was quite small due to the extensive purification process.

The preparation of supramolecular hybrid of GQDs and the maleimide-derivatized Pc was achieved by the non-covalent coordination (π-π stacking) of pristine GQDs to the Pc (2). Briefly: 2mg of pristine GQDs was added to 0.5mg of complex 2 (0.0052mmol) in 5mL of dry DMSO. The resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 2h; followed by stirring in the dark for 48h. A mixture of toluene, hexane and ethanol (1:1:9) was used to repeatedly wash the products so as to dispel the excess uncoordinated GQDs or complex 2. The solid products were air dried overnight and subsequently characterized. The nanoconjugate is represented as 2-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing procedures for the biothiols (Cys, Hcy and GSH)
ce:section.ce:para: The M-GQDs solutions were prepared in aqueous phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) and in DMSO/PBS (1:4v/v, 1×10−2 molL−1, pH 7.0) in the case of 2-GQDs. Note that DMSO/PBS (pH 7.0) solvent mixture was employed due to the solubility of complex 2 in DMSO and desired aqueous environment for the test analytes (Cys, Hcy or GSH). This was followed by the ultrasonic dispersion of weighed amounts of the probes to obtain 0.1 and 0.5mgmL−1 solutions of M-GQDs and 2-GQDs, respectively. Then 2.0mL of each probe solution was incubated in vials containing different known concentrations of aqueous solutions of Cys, Hcy and GSH (freshly prepared before use). The fluorescence emission spectra of the prepared M-GQDs and 2-GQDs were monitored after an equilibration time of 1min (before and after the addition of different concentrations of biothiols) at an excitation wavelength of 350 and 340nm, respectively. Maleimide moieties are known to react specifically with thiols (-SH) groups when the pH of the reaction mixture is between pH 6.5 and 7.5 [24]; GQDs PL properties are generally at optimum within pH 7.0–9.0, hence pH 7.0 was employed due to the desired sensing at physiological pH. Also in DMSO (and other polar solvents), thiol-maleimide Michael addition reaction has been described to occur very rapidly [19].

GQDs with amino functionality were initially synthesized by the incorporation of the poly-amino compound (PEI) unto the surface and edges of GQDs as reported previously [14,25]. The functionalization of GQDs with maleimide groups was achieved by the covalent amidation reaction between the -NH2 groups of the PEI-GQDs and the carboxylic groups (-COOH) of the maleimide compound via EDC/NHS chemistry (Scheme 1).

X-ray photoemission spectroscopic data (survey scan) of the as-synthesized M-GQDs revealed the presence of C, N and O as the prominent elements. The C1s, N1s and O1s peaks occurred at ca 284, 399 and 530 (eV), respectively ( Fig. 1A). Upon comparison of the survey spectrum of PEI-GQDs to that of the as-synthesized M-GQDs, similar elemental compositions are observed which is expected, but the intensities of the peaks corresponding to each of the C, N and O atomic percentages varied significantly with higher intensities for the M-GQDs (Fig. 1A). This observation confirms the conjugation of the PEI-GQDs to the maleimide units to form the M-GQDs. Further, the N1s and C1s signals were deconvoluted in order to confirm the changes in bonding states as a result of the amide coupling reaction. As shown in Fig. 1B(a), the N1s peaks for PEI-GQDs is composed of two peaks centered at 398.5 and 399.7eV due to -N-C- and –N-H bonds, respectively. The deconvoluted signal of as-synthesized M-GQDs fits into three components at 398.5, 399.7 and 401.1eV, Fig. 1B(b), with the emergence of a new peak (N-C=O) at 401.1eV further confirming a new bonding state due to the amide linkage [26]. This new bonding state was further verified by the presence of more C1s components for the M-GQDs as compared to the PEI-GQDs alone (Fig. 1C).

The AFM image of the M-GQDs showed that they have a uniform dispersion with a thickness of 0.72nm which denotes single layered GQDs across the line section shown on the image with a diameter of ~25nm ( Fig. 2A).

Transmission electron micrograph of the as-synthesized M-GQDs, Fig. 2B, shows monodispersed particles that are well separated with relatively wide size distribution ranging from 15 to 25nm (average diameter of ca 20nm), close to that determined using AFM at 25nm.

Dynamic light scattering experiments further gave insight into the changes in size of the GQDs upon conjugation to the maleimide moiety. Expectedly, the size of PEI-GQDs increased from ca 10nm (average size) to 19.5nm to form M-GQDs (Fig. 2C). This result is in close agreement with size distribution range acquired from TEM analysis.

FTIR spectra of the PEI-GQDs before and after conjugation to the maleimide unit revealed broad intense vibrations at 3320 and 3316cm−1 ( Fig. 3a,b) attributed to the N-H stretching vibrations of C-NH and residual O-H stretching vibrations from the C-OH groups, respectively. The signature secondary and primary amide bond peaks were observed for the M-GQDs at 1566 and 1634cm−1, Fig. 3b, which overlapped with the –C˭C- backbone of the graphene structure of the PEI-GQDs (1642cm−1, Fig. 3a) and further demonstrated the successful formation of the desired M-GQDs.

Raman spectrum obtained for the M-GQDs displayed slight shifts in the characteristic D (disordered carbon atoms at the edges) and G (sp2 bonded carbon atoms)-bands as compared to the PEI-GQDs ( Fig. 4). The observed shifts and increase in the D: G ratio (ca 0.95 for M-GQDs and 0.52 for PEI-GQDs) is an indication of defects introduction upon conjugation which further confirmed the formation of a new nanocomplex of M-GQDs.

The fluorescence properties of the maleimide-functionalized GQDs (M-GQDs) showed a marked difference from that of the precursor PEI-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence emission intensity of the M-GQDs was weakened upon the attachment of the maleimide group and a corresponding shift in the fluorescence (FL) intensity peak from 448 (for PEI-GQDs) to 452nm (for M-GQDs) at similar excitation wavelength of 350nm (Fig. 5A inset and Table 1). Maleimide groups are known to effectively quench the fluorescence of fluorophores since their low lying n-π* state facilitates the non-radiative pathways for excited state decay [27]. Hence, maleimide attachment to electron rich species or donor fluorophores would result in electron(s) transfer to the electron-deficient maleimide ring which then results into the weakening or quenching of the donor molecules’ emission as observed in this study [27]. The observed weak fluorescence emission of the M-GQDs upon covalent conjugation to the maleimide units clearly demonstrated the quenching effects of maleimide groups. To gain more insight and substantiate our observation, fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime of the as-synthesized M-GQDs were evaluated (see supporting information for details). In agreement with the spectrofluorimetric results, the FL quantum yield showed a huge decrease upon maleimide attachment to GQDs by about 93% as well as a decrease in the lifetime from 9.7 to 1.50ns (Table 1), which corresponds to the observed quenching of the GQDs emission upon attachment to the maleimide group. Fluorescence lifetimes were found to be of single exponential decay.

Scheme 2 shows the synthesis pathways adopted for the preparation of complex 2. The Pc complex (2) was further characterized by 1H NMR, MALDI-TOF mass, and FT-IR spectroscopic techniques, and elemental analysis.

The FT-IR spectrum of the maleimide-derivatized ZnPc (2) displayed significantly broadened peak (near 3000cm−1) due to the maleimide groups attached to the Pc ring ( Fig. 6b). Specific vibration of carbonyls of the maleimide groups is centered at 1710cm−1 which is clearly absent in the spectrum of the precursor Pc (complex 1) (Fig. 6a). The aromatic –C˭C- vibration of the Pc ring observed at 1605cm−1 for complex 1 (employed as a precursor for complex 2) was still retained in complex 2 but with a shift to 1647cm−1. This spectral signature is characteristic of the presence of the maleimide groups on the Pc ring and further proved the formation of the desired Pc complex.

1H NMR spectrum for complex 2 displayed peaks centered at δ 8.97–8.87 (m, 3H), 8.40 (s, 1H), 8.44 (d, 2H), 7.27-7.14 (m, 6H) which accounted for the 12 protons corresponding to the Pc skeleton protons. Signals from the maleimide protons on the Pc ring were observed as multiplet at δ (ppm) 6.24-6.18, which upon integration resulted in 8 protons (Fig. S1). The MALDI-TOF-MS characterization of the Pc complex 2 gave definitive proof as to the synthesis of the maleimide-derivatized Pc which is reported for the first time in this study. The expected and the obtained mass to charge ratio (m/z) for complex 2 are 956 m/z and 957 m/z [M+H]+, respectively (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the elemental analysis results were close to the expected percentage elemental compositions of the complex, further confirming the purity of the obtained Pc (complex 2).

The conversion of complex 1 (with terminal amino groups) to 2 (with maleimide groups) (Scheme 2) was verified and confirmed by the observed changes in the ground-state absorptions of the Pc complexes. The Q-band of 1 (in DMSO) was observed at 723nm which is highly red-shifted due to the presence of electron donating terminal amino groups (-NH2) on the Pc ring ( Fig. 7a)[28]. Consequently, the transformation of the amino groups on the terminal positions of 1 to maleimide units resulted in a blue-shifted Q-band at 699nm, Fig. 7b, Table 1. The observed blue-shift in the spectra of 2 relative to 1 is due to the conversion of the electron donating amino groups to the electron withdrawing (deficient) maleimide groups (maleimide moieties are known to be electron deficient due to their low lying n-π* state [27]). Within the concentration range of ca 1×10−6 to 1×10−5 mol L−1 in DMSO, 2 did not show any signs of aggregation as Beer's law was obeyed at this concentration range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pristine GQDs-zinc tetramaleimido Pc analogue (2-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Complex 2 was coordinated to pristine GQDs via the non-covalent interaction method (π-π stacking) to obtained 2-GQDs nanocomposite as depicted in Scheme 3.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image recorded for the nanocomposite (Fig. 2D) displayed evidence of aggregation. Non-covalent (π-π stacking) interaction of GQDs and Pcs usually results in the adsorption of the Pcs unto the larger GQDs’ graphene sheets and the interaction between adjacent Pcs could possibly lead to aggregates formation in the nanocomposite. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments indicated an increase in the hydrodynamic sizes of the 2-GQDs nanocomposite (~24.4nm) (Fig. 2E) relative to pristine GQDs alone which have an average size of ~8nm (figure not shown). The adsorption of 2 unto the pristine GQDs surface via π-π stacking interaction was further demonstrated by the observed increase in sizes from the DLS experiments.

FTIR analysis of 2-GQDs (Fig. 6d) further provided concrete evidence supporting the formation of the conjugate. As shown in Fig. 6, the broad peak (centered at ~ 3352cm−1) of the hydroxyl groups on the GQDs surface or edges, was observed for both pristine GQDs (Fig. 6c) and 2-GQDs (Fig. 6d). The –C˭O stretching vibrations ca 1647cm−1 for complex 2 and pristine GQDs was still observed for 2-GQDs with some shift to 1605cm−1. Definitive signature vibrations of the carbonyls of the maleimide units appeared at 1768 and 1710cm−1 in 2-GQDs conjugate. The latter was also observed for complex 2 (before coordination to GQDs). The peak at 1768cm−1 was not distinct in complex 2 due to the spectral broadening (Fig. 6b). The peak occurring at 1488cm−1 for 2-GQDs is attributed to –C˭C- and it is present in pristine GQDs (with a shift to 1434cm−1) and complex 2, Fig. 6(c,d). These results further show the successful formation of the GQDs composites with the Pc (2) in a non-covalently bound interaction. It is known that shifts in IR bands confirm structural changes [29].

Ground state absorption spectrum of 2-GQDs exhibited specific absorptions corresponding to 2 with Q-band at 708nm, which is red-shifted relative to 2 alone (Fig. 7c, Table 1). The intense absorption in the blue region (<400nm) in 2-GQDs spectrum is due to the absorption of the GQDs (Fig. 7d). Hence, typical absorption characteristics of GQDs and 2 could be appreciated in the spectrum of the nanocomposite further attesting to its formation. Red shifts in similar macrocycles such as porphyrins have been observed in porphyrins-graphene oxide nanoconjugates [30].

The fluorescence properties of the newly synthesized Pc complex (2) were investigated prior to coordination with GQDs. As shown in Table 1, the fluorescence quantum yield of 2 was found to be comparatively low (compared to ZnPc standard fluorescence at 0.2 in DMSO [31]). The same applies to the lifetime. This observation is ascribed to the presence of the maleimide groups on the Pc periphery and due to their low lying n-π* state which disrupt radiative recombination, hence suppression of fluorescence emission as discussed above. Furthermore, fluorescence behaviour of 2 upon coordination with pristine GQDs was investigated so as to have insights on the changes in the optical properties of the Pc upon interaction with GQDs. At an excitation wavelength of 633nm for 2-GQDs, the fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of 2 in 2-GQDs nanocomposite increased upon coordination to the pristine GQDs when compared to 2 alone (Table 1). This result was also reflected by corresponding increases in the FL lifetime of the nanocomposite (using similar excitation source (LDP-670nm) as for the Pc alone). The increases are however, small. Fluorescence lifetimes were found to be of single exponential decay.

Fluorescence quantum yields and lifetimes of pristine GQDs upon coordination to the maleimide-derivatized Pc (2) were evaluated as a means to understanding the interaction and possible quenching dynamics of the pristine GQDs emission within the nanocomposite. At similar excitation wavelength (340nm, wavelength of maximum emission of pristine GQDs), 2-GQDs supramolecular hybrid displayed weak fluorescence emission (Fig. 5B) compared to pristine GQDs. This was accompanied by a decrease in the fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) and lifetimes of the GQDs upon coordination with 2 corresponding to the quenching of the GQDs fluorescence (Table 1 and Fig. 5B). Interactions between GQDs and Pcs or porphyrins have been reported to result in non-radiative recombination of the GQDs excitons with a consequent decrease in fluorescence of GQDs [32]. Hence, the observed fluorescence quenching is an indication of energy loss or transfer to the interacting macrocycles as previously reported [32].

The quenching of GQDs in the presence of complex 2 could be due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) which occurs between GQDs and Pcs [33]. An evaluation of the possible energy transfer via FRET was done by calculating the FRET efficiency (Eff) using eqn. (2) in supporting information) and was found to be ca 67.7% (Table 1), thus demonstrating relatively high interaction between GQDs and 2. It should be noted that the calculated FRET efficiency values are only estimates, since there are a wide variety of factors which influence the decrease in GQDs emission in addition to FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the maleimide-derivatized probes for biothiols detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission intensity of the GQDs was weakened (Fig. 5A) upon conjugation to the maleimide units via a fluorescence “turn OFF” process usually occasioned by the attachments of maleimide units to fluorophores as discussed above [15–18,27]. Conversely, the PL intensity of M-GQDs was gradually restored upon the addition of different concentrations of Cys, Hcy or GSH, respectively ( Fig. 8A and B) as test analytes. When different concentrations of Cys, Hcy or GSH were added to complex 2, there was no significant increase in the fluorescence of 2 (exciting where 2 absorbs, figure not shown). This could be due to the low fluorescence of ZnPc derivatives such as complex 2. Hence complex 2 alone cannot be used to sense biothiols even though the maleimide units on complex 2 interact with these biothiols. However, the interaction of the respective biothiols with 2-GQDs resulted in the observed recovery of the quenched emission of GQDs (exciting where GQDs absorb) which was modulated by the sequential increase in the concentrations of Cys, Hcy or GSH, respectively ( Fig. 9, Cys shown as an example). Thus, complex 2 can be employed for indirect detection of biothiols in the presence of GQDs (the latter provides a stronger signal compared to the former).

In order to evaluate the fluorescence “turn ON” sensitivity of the probes (M-GQDs and 2-GQDs), the recovered fluorescence intensities in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of Cys, Hcy or GSH were evaluated using Eq. (1) [34].
(1)
∆
F
F
0
=
1
+
K
[
B
]
where K is the fluorescence enhancement constant and [B] is the concentration of biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH). ∆F/F0 which denotes fluorescence enhancement is applied instead of F0/F normally used to indicate a fluorescence quenching process. K values were used to assess the sensitivity of the nanoprobes. In order to establish the sensitive nature of the probes, the limits of detection (LODs) for the biothiols were evaluated from the plots shown in Figs. 8B and 9 insets, and using 3δ/K (where δ is the standard deviation of blank measurement (n=14) and K is the slope of the calibration graphs, see Supporting information for details). For M-GQDs, the [(F-F0)/F0] values were plotted against the biothiols (Cys, Hcy and GSH) concentrations and the linear ranges were obtained to be 0.5–120nmolL−1 for Cys; 0.5–150nmol'L−1 for Hcy and 1.0–200nmolL−1 for GSH, respectively (Fig. 8B inset, as an example). Interestingly, the LODs were evaluated to be as low as 0.30nmolL−1, 0.45nmol L−1 and 0.86nmolL−1 for Cys, Hcys and GSH, respectively ( Table 2), when M-GQDs is employed as a probe for biothiols detection. Linear ranges and LODs for 2-GQDs probe are shown in Table 2 [12,14,16,18,35–42], with LODs of 0.85, 1.42 and 3.2nmolL−1 for Cys, Hcy and GSH, respectively. It follows from the obtained detection limits that M-GQDs assay is highly sensitive with the lowest detection limits compared to 2-GQDs for biothiols sensing and other GQDs and non-GQDs based nanoprobes reported to date as shown in Table 2 (with the exception of exonuclease III and DNA [42]). The LODs for these biothiols using M-GQDs is better than those we reported for PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag blend [14], where the values were 1.5nmolL−1, 3.0nmolL−1 and 4.5nmolL−1 for Cys, Hcy and GSH, respectively (Table 2), showing the advantage of using maleimide as a specific substituent for biothiols.

Maleimide groups are known to react specifically with thiol groups (–SH) which exist in the side-chains of biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH) residues [24] via Michael addition reaction. As discussed earlier, the attachment of maleimide units (which are known electron accepting groups) to the electron donating GQDs resulted in the “turn-OFF” state of the M-GQDs. This could be due to either Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) or photo-induced electron (PET) pathways [34] from the GQDs core to the maleimide domains. During the sensing process, the interaction of maleimide-functionalized GQDs (m-GQDs) probe with the respective biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH) resulted in the conversion of the double bond of the maleimide ring to a saturated maleimide ring via Michael addition reaction ( Scheme 4), hence destroying the quenching ability of the maleimide domains attached to the GQDs by the deactivation of the ICT and/or PET process(es), further leading to the fluorescence “turn-ON” state of the GQDs. Thus, the biothiols addition to the maleimide moieties (which occurs rapidly due to the maleimide ring strain) removes π-electrons [19], hence n-π* transition is destroyed or affected and the quenching ability of the maleimide becomes passivated. Fluorescence lifetime experiments are highly sensitive and insightful during fluorimetric processes, hence were further employed to verify the off-on process of the probe (m-GQDs) in the presence of the biothiols. As shown in (Table S1), fluorescence lifetimes increased upon the addition of increasing concentrations of Cys to the M-GQDs probe. Similar changes in the fluorescence lifetimes were observed when Hcy and GSH were separately titrated with M-GQDs probe (Table S1). These results gave a definite rationalization of such high “turn-ON” process which is largely ascribed to the blocking of the ICT or PET pathways due to the conjugation of the thiols to the maleimide units attached to the GQDs.

On the other hand, the observed fluorescence “off-on” of the 2-GQDs probe in the presence of the biothiols occurred as a result of the maleimide substituents appended to the Pc ring via the rapid Michael addition of the biothiols as similarly discussed above. The specific mechanism of the recovery of the fluorescence emission of the GQDs coordinated to 2 in the presence of biothiols may be based on two rational theories. Firstly, the interaction of complex 2 with GQDs resulted in the quenching of the GQDs fluorescence (Fig. 5B) which could be based on either energy or electron transfer from the GQDs fluorophore to the electron deficient 2 due to the low lying n-π* transition of the maleimide ring attached directly to the Pc ring of 2. Consequently, the Michael addition of the biothiols to the probe (which occurs rapidly and selectively with 2 as a component of the probe) results in the saturation (removal of π bonds) of the maleimide rings. UV–vis spectra of 2 in the presence of Cys (50 and 100nmolL−1) resulted in changes of the ground-state absorption of 2 with a blue-shift in the Q-band upon addition of Cys which confirmed the specific interaction between 2 and the biothiols ( Fig. 10A). This interaction could disrupt the electronic or energy interaction between the GQDs and 2 thus leading to the enhancement of the GQDs emission within the conjugate which was further confirmed by changes in the lifetime of the GQDs before and after the addition of the respective biothiols to the probe (Fig. 10B). Secondly, the introduced biothiols could be incorporated as spacers between the GQDs and 2 since both are non-covalently coordinated, hence the attachment of the biothiols to 2 (forming the thioether-Pc adduct) (Scheme 3) could largely result in the dissociation of the 2-GQDs components leading to disrupted interaction and consequent fluorescence “turn-ON” process. Further, Cys solution of different concentrations was added to the GQDs alone, but no changes in the fluorescence properties were observed (Fig. S3), thereby demonstrating that the “turn-ON” fluorescence is effectively unaffected by the biothiols and purely arises from the Michael addition of the biothiols to 2.

The selectivity of the M-GQDs and 2-GQDs probes which involves their ability to preferentially recognize biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH) without interferences from other molecules was studied. The restored fluorescence emission intensities relative to that acquired in the presence of some proteins (bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, and cytochrome c) and α-amino acids (glycine, lysine, histidine, tryptophan and DL-lactic acid etc.) under optimized conditions are remarkably different as shown in Fig. 11. The difference in the recovered fluorescence intensities for the respective biothiols and other α-amino acids and proteins is a demonstration of the high specificity and selectivity for biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH) recognition by the fabricated probes. This result further contributes to the analytical figures of merits of biothiols quantification by the maleimide-derivatized GQDs and Pc systems. It is noteworthy to consider that the excellent selectivity results obtained herein originates from the rapid and specific Michael addition reactions propensity of the maleimide bearing M-GQDs and complex 2 which expediently ensures their specific and selective interaction with thiols [15–18,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery studies (analytical applicability of the probes)
ce:section.ce:para: Simulated (spiked) samples with known concentrations of Cys, Hcy or GSH were subjected to analysis by the prepared probes following the procedures devised in this study. The recovery results obtained further demonstrated the usability of the probes for biothiols quantification in real samples. The analytical precision (batch to batch analysis) is usually demonstrated in terms of the relative standard deviation (RSD) (also known as percent coefficient of variation, % CV). As we show in Table 3, the quantified Cys, Hcy or GSH (recovery percent (%) against known amount in the spiked samples) and the relative standard deviation (RSD) evaluated from three replicate measurements, n=3 demonstrates the precision and reproducibility of our assays. Comparing the individual probes ability to detect the biothiols reproducibly, we see that the recoveries were higher for the M-GQDs probe compared to the 2-GQDs probe (Table 3). This could be ascribed to the combined advantage of excellent aqueous dispersibility and the direct attachment of the maleimide unit to the GQDs surface in the former (M-GQDs) over the later (2-GQDs) since the thiol-maleimide reaction is rapid and stable in aqueous environments [23]. Notwithstanding, the feasibility of biothiols recognition in simulated samples was further demonstrated by the designed probes.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based nanoprobes using maleimide-functionalized GQDs (M-GQDs) and zinc phthalocyanine for rapid, sensitive and selective fluorescence “ off-on ” detection of biothiols. The nanoprobes contain maleimide units which are highly selective and reactive towards biothiols via the well-known Michael addition reaction as the underlying sensing mechanism. The M-GQDs were found to demonstrate higher detection sensitivity as compared to its Pc-GQDs counterpart which is ascribed to the direct attachment of the maleimide units to the M-GQDs surface. Several α-amino acids and proteins did not affect the detection sensitivities of the nanoprobes. Hence, novel nanoprobes capable of nanomolar quantification of biothiols in aqueous medium and at physiological pH have been developed in this study.
There are no potential conflicts of interest with regard to this manuscript. The funders have agreed to publication. The research does not involve human participants and/or Animals.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.01.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
